Addition is commutative , meaning that one can change the order of the terms in a sum, but still get the same result.
Addition is associative , which means that when three or more numbers are added together, the order of operations does not change the result.

annotations.label: Commutativity and associativity of rational addition

anchor.snippet: The commutativity and associativity of rational addition are easy consequences of the laws of integer arithmetic.
The sum of real numbers a and b is defined element by element
A real number is essentially defined to be the limit of a Cauchy sequence of rationals
Complex numbers are added by adding the real and imaginary parts of the summands.
the sum of two complex numbers A and B , interpreted as points of the complex plane, is the point X obtained by building a parallelogram
In the special case where the order does not matter, the composition operator is sometimes called addition.
The sum of two vectors is obtained by adding their individual coordinates
Matrix addition is defined for two matrices of the same dimensions.
In modular arithmetic , the set of available numbers is restricted to a finite subset of the integers
The set of integers modulo 2 has just two elements
where the sum of two angle measures is often taken to be their sum as real numbers modulo 2π
The general theory of abstract algebra allows an "addition" operation to be any associative and commutative operation on a set.
Linear combinations combine multiplication and summation
A far-reaching generalization of the addition of natural numbers is the addition of ordinal numbers and cardinal numbers in set theory.
Unlike most addition operations, the addition of ordinal numbers is not commutative.
Addition of cardinal numbers, however, is a commutative operation closely related to the disjoint union operation.
In category theory , disjoint union is seen as a particular case of the coproduct operation
Subtraction can be thought of as a kind of addition
Multiplication can be thought of as repeated addition .
In the real and complex numbers, addition and multiplication can be interchanged by the exponential function
multiplication operations always distribute over addition; this requirement is formalized in the definition of a ring
